title: Synapcity Knowledge Graph
subtitle: Designing a source-backed memory system for teams that lose decisions in scattered tools
description: A product architecture case study about modeling team knowledge as source-backed decisions, questions, and evidence instead of another document repository.

images.alt: Synapcity mind map exploring team memory and knowledge graph concepts
images.caption: Early concept map for decisions, sources, people, and follow-up questions.

images.alt: Synapcity task interface for organizing follow-up work
images.caption: Task surface for turning recovered context into next actions.

images.alt: Synapcity widget layout for surfacing contextual knowledge
images.caption: Widget experiments for bringing context back into active workflows.

sections.title: Why team memory breaks down
paragraphs: Teams rarely lose knowledge all at once. They lose it through small context leaks: decisions buried in chat, undocumented trade-offs, unresolved questions, and onboarding paths that depend on whoever remembers the history.

Synapcity explores how to make that context recoverable without asking people to maintain a second system by hand. The product thesis is that teams need source-backed memory, not another place to write notes.

pullQuote.text: The hard part is not storing knowledge. The hard part is making the right context reappear when a team needs it.

sections.title: The questions we had to answer

decisions.title: What counts as knowledge worth preserving?
decisions.teaser: A capture-everything system becomes noise almost immediately.
decisions.answer: The model focuses on decisions, source material, people, open questions, and follow-up tasks. Raw messages can be evidence, but they are not the primary product object.
alternatives: Index every message and rely on search ranking
Ask users to manually curate canonical documents
Treat every document, message, and task as the same object type

decisions.title: How should permissions work across team memory?
decisions.teaser: Knowledge tools fail quickly if private context leaks into broad search results.
decisions.answer: Permissions are inherited from source systems and carried into derived artifacts. If a user cannot access the source, they should not see generated summaries or connected nodes that reveal its contents.
alternatives: Manual labels on every captured artifact
Separate indexes per source with no cross-linking

decisions.title: Where should AI sit in the workflow?
decisions.teaser: Generated summaries are useful only when users can inspect what they are based on.
decisions.answer: AI supports clustering, naming decision threads, and suggesting missing questions. Every generated artifact needs source links and a visible confidence boundary.
alternatives: A chat-first interface for all interactions
A purely manual map of team knowledge

callout.text: The product becomes more useful when knowledge is modeled as connected claims, decisions, people, and evidence rather than as a folder of documents.

designDecision.title: Source-Backed Knowledge Nodes
designDecision.description: Every durable piece of knowledge is represented as a node with links back to the source material that produced it.
designDecision.why: Teams need to trust the output. Source-backed nodes make generated context auditable instead of mysterious.

constraints.title: Source material is messy
constraints.description: Relevant context can live in chat, docs, tickets, meetings, and people's heads.

constraints.title: Access control is product-critical
constraints.description: Search and summarization cannot reveal private source material through indirect references.

constraints.title: Users should not need a new habit
constraints.description: The product needs to recover context from existing workflows before asking users to create new artifacts.

sections.title: What we chose not to do

tradeoffs.title: No full project management layer
tradeoffs.whatWeChose: Focus on knowledge recovery and decision context.
tradeoffs.whatItCost: Tasks and delivery planning still live in dedicated tools.
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: If the knowledge graph starts exposing workflow gaps that existing tools cannot represent.

tradeoffs.title: No automatic truth source
tradeoffs.whatWeChose: Show source-backed context with confidence instead of declaring a single canonical answer.
tradeoffs.whatItCost: Users still need judgment when sources disagree.
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: When the system can model provenance, freshness, and authority well enough to rank truth claims responsibly.

milestones.title: Problem framing
milestones.description: Interviewed operators and product teams about where decisions disappear and how they recover them.

milestones.title: Knowledge node model
milestones.description: Modeled decisions, evidence, people, and open questions as connected objects.

milestones.title: Workflow experiments
milestones.description: Tested whether widgets, task surfaces, and summaries helped teams recover context faster.

metrics.label: Object types
metrics.description: Decision, evidence, person, question, and task nodes in the prototype model.

metrics.label: Source links
metrics.description: Target coverage for generated artifacts that must link back to source material.

metrics.label: Workflow surfaces
metrics.description: Mind map, task view, and contextual widgets used to test retrieval patterns.

metrics.label: Permission boundary
metrics.description: Derived artifacts inherit access from their source systems.

reflections.insight: A knowledge product has to earn trust through provenance. Users are more willing to use generated context when they can inspect where it came from.
reflections.transferable: AI-assisted products should make source inspection part of the core interaction, not an afterthought.

reflections.insight: The first useful unit is often a decision, not a document. Decisions carry enough context to connect people, evidence, and next actions.
reflections.transferable: When designing knowledge systems, choose object types that match how teams recover context.

sections.title: The work ahead
sections.intro: The next phase is about validating whether the knowledge model helps real teams recover context with less manual curation.

items.title: Source connector tests
items.description: Prototype a small set of source integrations and verify permission inheritance end to end.

items.title: Decision thread UX
items.description: Refine how related evidence and open questions are grouped around a decision.

items.title: Trust controls
items.description: Expose confidence, provenance, and freshness signals directly in the interface.

problem: Teams lose decisions, evidence, and unresolved questions across scattered tools faster than they can recover them later.
solution: Model team knowledge as source-backed decisions, evidence, people, tasks, and open questions with inherited permission boundaries.
role: Product discovery, knowledge model design, and prototype architecture.
proof: The case study validates decision-centered object types, source links, workflow surfaces, and permission inheritance targets.
impact: Source-backed knowledge modeling helps teams recover context without turning every message or document into another manual repository.
challenges: Separating durable knowledge from noisy source material
Making generated context inspectable and trustworthy
Preserving source-system access boundaries in derived artifacts
learnings: A knowledge product has to earn trust through provenance.
The first useful unit is often a decision, not a document.
